b. **Request Type**: The direct prompt explicitly asks for a code review with feedback on code quality, best practices, potential bugs/issues, performance considerations, security concerns, and test coverage.

c. **Key Information**:
   - PR adds a new MIRNA_DEGRADATION project scaffold
   - Author: cmungall
   - 645 additions, 0 deletions across 6 files
   - Files include project documentation, gene lists, and rendered HTML
   - Focus is on target-directed microRNA degradation (TDMD)

d. **Main Tasks**:
   - Review the 6 changed files for code quality and best practices
   - Check for potential bugs or issues
   - Assess performance and security considerations
   - Evaluate test coverage
   - Provide constructive feedback

e. **Plan of Action**:
   - Read and analyze each of the 6 changed files
   - Check against repository guidelines in CLAUDE.md
   - Review file structure and naming conventions
   - Assess data quality and format consistency
   - Check for any security or performance issues
   - Provide comprehensive review feedback via comment update

f. **Limitations**: I should be able to complete this review fully as it's analyzing existing files and providing feedback.
</analysis>

I'll review this pull request and provide comprehensive feedback. Let me start by creating a todo list and analyzing the changes.

input.body: ### PR Review: microRNA degradation project scaffold **Review Tasks:** - [ ] Read repository guidelines (CLAUDE.md) - [ ] Analyze project documentation (`projects/MIRNA_DEGRADATION.md`) - [ ] Review gene data files (`genes.csv`, `fetch_queue.csv`) - [ ] Check source documentation (`sources.md`) - [ ] Examine rendered HTML files - [ ] Assess code quality, best practices, and potential issues - [ ] Provide comprehensive feedback Starting analysis... [View job run](https://github.com/ai4curation/ai-gene-review/actions/runs/23989679840)

content.text: Now let me read the repository guidelines and then examine each of the changed files.

content.content:
1→# AI instructions for ai-gene-review
     2→
     3→## Project Overview
     4→
     5→This project is for managing AI reviews of gene annotations. The overall goal is to both
     6→review existing GO annotations using strict defined criteria, and to summarize the resulting
     7→high quality annotations.
     8→
     9→## Repository layout
    10→
    11→src/
    12→  ai_gene_review/
    13→    schema/
    14→      gene_review.yaml <-- linkml schema for review docs
    15→genes/
    16→  human/
    17→    CFAP300/
    18→      CFAP300-notes.md <-- keep your notes here
    19→      CFAP300-ai-review.yaml <-- the output of your AI review
    20→      CFAP300-notes.md <-- your own notes, edit this, use it like a journal and keep appending.
    21→      CFAP300-uniprot.txt <-- the uniprot record downloaded from the UniProt website (do not edit this file)
    22→      CFAP300-goa.tsv <-- the GO annotation downloaded from the QuickGO API (do not edit this file, no need to view it)
    23→      CFAP300-deep-research.md <-- the deep research for the gene review (do not edit this file, it is automatically generated for you)
    24→      CFAP300-citations.md <-- the citations for the gene review (do not edit this file)
    25→      CFAP300-bioinformatics/ <-- the folder for any bioinformatics analyses (create this if necessary)
    26→      CFAP300-predictions-review.yaml <-- review of computational predictions NOT in GOA (optional)
    27→    <other genes>/
    28→      ...
    29→  worm/
    30→    lrx-1/
    31→  SCHPO/  <-- other orgs use uniprot species codes
    32→      ...
    33→publications/
    34→  PMID_123456.md <-- cached publication text (this should be available for you, DO NOT EDIT)
    35→reactome/
    36→  R-HSA-NNNNNN.md <- cached reactome entry
    37→
    38→You can regenerate the derived files by running commands like:
    39→
    40→```
    41→just fetch-gene human CFAP300 
    42→```
    43→
    44→This makes the uniprot, goa, and publications files. It will also create a stub for the ai-review.yaml if it doesn't exist.
    45→
    46→After making edits to the ai-review.yaml file, you can validate the file by running:
    47→
    48→```
    49→just validate human CFAP300
    50→```
    51→
    52→This ensures that the yaml file is consistent with the schema in `src/ai_gene_review/schema/gene_review.yaml`, and also
    53→that it implements best practices.
    54→
    55→To validate all files, run:
    56→
    57→```
    58→just validate-all
    59→```
    60→
    61→files will be written to reports/validation-all.tsv 
    62→
    63→* All gene symbols follow the standard nomenclature for that species. For human, should be HGNC symbols.
    64→
    65→## Gene Review Guidelines
    66→
    67→Your job is to review existing annotations for a gene, following GO guidelines, and synthesize these with what is known in the literature,
    68→and what can be inferred bioinformatically. The general structure of the review is as follows:
    69→
    70→```yaml
    71→id: <uniprot id>
    72→gene_symbol: <HGNC symbol>
    73→taxon:
    74→  id: <NCBI taxon id>
    75→  label: <taxon label>
    76→description: <your own summary of the gene>
    77→references:
    78→  <all relevant reference, including both literature and bioinformatics sources; includes all but not limited to those used in existing_annotations>
    79→existing_annotations:
    80→  <review of existing annotations, using the GO guidelines; should include all annotations from goa.csv>
    81→core_functions:
    82→  <your own summary of the core functions of the gene>
    83→proposed_new_terms:
    84→  <suggestions for new GO terms>
    85→suggested_questions:
    86→  <suggested questions for experts>
    87→suggested_experiments:
    88→  <suggested experiments to elucidate gene function>
    89→```
    90→
    91→See the schema and existing files for more details.
    92→
    93→When creating a new review, always make sure files are in place:
    94→
    95→`just fetch-gene <organism> <gene>`
    96→
    97→This creates the uniprot, goa files, and a stub for the gene review.
    98→
    99→The next thing is for you to do deep research on that gene and make a GENE-notes.md file, with citations inline (e.g [PMID:12345 title, "<supporting text>"]). In some cases this will have been done ahead of time for you.
   100→
   101→## Reviewing existing annotations
   102→
   103→You should start with the ai-review.yaml file, where stubs are created for you (from `just fetch-gene <organism> <gene>`). Any PMID cited should be in the publications folder, e.g. `publications/PMID_123456.md`. Review these.
   104→
   105→A deep research file should already be present in the gene folder, e.g. `genes/SPECIES/GENE/GENE-deep-research-PROVIDER.md` (where PROVIDER is openai, falcon, perplexity, etc.). This should contain a summary of the literature, and any other information you found useful for the review. There may be irrelevant information in the citations, that is OK. If this is not present, you can generate one using:
   106→
   107→```bash
   108→just deep-research SPECIES GENE --provider perplexity
   109→```
   110→
   111→This will create a file like `GENE-deep-research-perplexity.md`. You can also make your own notes in `genes/SPECIES/GENE/GENE-notes.md`.
   112→
   113→NEVER write your own content and name it `-deep-research-{provider}.md`. If deep research fails, document in `-notes.md` or `-deep-research-manual.md` instead.
   114→
   115→Be aware that many GO terms are over-annotations. You should also not take existing annotations as gospel, whether experimental or bioinformatic.
   116→always use a holistic synthesized picture of the gene function that you have obtained from your research.
   117→
   118→```yaml
   119→ActionEnum:
   120→    permissible_values:
   121→      ACCEPT:
   122→        description: Accept the existing annotation as-is, no modifications, and retain as representing the core function of the gene
   123→      KEEP_AS_NON_CORE:
   124→        description: Keep the existing annotation as-is, but mark it as non-core. For pleiotropic genes, this may be the developmental
   125→          processes, or other processes that are not the core function of the gene.
   126→      REMOVE:
   127→        description: Remove the existing annotation, as it is unlikely to be correct based on combined evidence
   128→      MODIFY:
   129→        description: The essence of the annotation is sound, but there are better terms to use (use in combination with proposed_replacement_terms).
   130→          if the term is too general, then MODIFY should be used, with a proposed replacement term for the correct specific function.
   131→          sometimes terms can also be overly specific and contorted, so in some cases you might want to generalize
   132→      MARK_AS_OVER_ANNOTATED:
   133→        description: The term is not entirely wrong, but likely represents an over-annotation of the gene
   134→      UNDECIDED:
   135→        description: The annotation is not clear, and the reviewer is not sure what to do with it. ALWAYS USE THIS IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO ACCESS
   136→          RELEVANT PUBLICATIONS
   137→      NEW:
   138→        
   139→```      
   140→
   141→## Tools
   142→
   143→Use the OLS MCP to find relevant ontology terms, if the terms you need are not in existing_annotations.
   144→
   145→## Curation guidelines
   146→
   147→Avoid the term `protein binding`, this doesn't tell us anything about the actual function. Instead find a more
   148→informative MF term (e.g for adapter function)
   149→
   150→## Isoform and Negation Tracking
   151→
   152→The system tracks isoform-specific GO annotations and NOT (negated) annotations:
   153→
   154→```yaml
   155→existing_annotations:
   156→# Isoform-specific annotation
   157→- term:
   158→    id: GO:0045892
   159→    label: negative regulation of DNA-templated transcription
   160→  evidence_type: IDA
   161→  original_reference_id: PMID:9815658
   162→  isoform: P19544-1  # UniProt isoform ID - only present for isoform-specific annotations
   163→  review:
   164→    summary: ...
   165→
   166→# NOT annotation (negated)
   167→- term:
   168→    id: GO:0045893
   169→    label: positive regulation of DNA-templated transcription
   170→  evidence_type: IDA
   171→  original_reference_id: PMID:9815658
   172→  negated: true  # This is a NOT annotation
   173→  isoform: P19544-1
   174→  review:
   175→    summary: WT1 does NOT activate transcription...
   176→```
   177→
   178→- **`isoform`**: UniProt isoform ID (e.g., `P19544-1`) - indicates what isoform was tested
   179→- **`negated`**: Set to `true` for NOT annotations (e.g., `NOT|involved_in` qualifier in GOA)
   180→
   181→**Important**: Just because an annotation was made on a specific isoform doesn't mean the function is isoform-specific.
   182→The `isoform` field indicates what was tested, not necessarily what is unique to that isoform.
   183→
   184→To backfill isoform info on existing reviews:
   185→```bash
   186→just backfill-isoforms human WT1
   187→just backfill-isoforms-organism human  # all genes in organism
   188→```
   189→
   190→See `docs/isoform_tracking.md` for full documentation and `projects/ISOFORMS.md` for genes with notable isoform-specific functions.
   191→
   192→## Bioinformatics analyses
   193→
   194→In some cases, it may be useful to do additional bioinformatics analyses. To validate gene function. Here are some guidelines:
   195→
   196→For a gene-specific analysis, the folder is `<genes/specues/GENE>-bioinformatics/`. You should make this be reproducible. You can do a `uv init`
   197→here and `uv add` for any packages you need.
   198→
   199→Never hardcode the results of the script, it's OK for a script to return inconclusive results. Never ever make up results.
   200→
   201→**IMPORTANT**: DO NOT write scripts that just tell users to go to a website. If you cannot programmatically call a tool (e.g., requires API keys, browser interaction, or authentication you don't have), then:
   202→1. Be honest about this limitation upfront
   203→2. Write scripts that can parse/process the output from the tool if the user runs it manually
   204→3. Never write misleading code that pretends to do something it doesn't actually do
   205→
   206→You can summarize the results of the bioinformatics analyses in the `<GENE>-bioinformatics/RESULTS.md` file. This can be referenced as any other reference
   207→as e.g `file:<SPECIES>/<GENE>/bioinformatics/RESULTS.md`.
   208→
   209→## Reviewing Computational Predictions
   210→
   211→The `GENE-predictions-review.yaml` file reviews computational/ML predictions that are NOT
   212→in the curated GOA/UniProt annotations. This is separate from `existing_annotations` in the
   213→main review file.
   214→
   215→Use the `PredictionReview` class (validated with `-C PredictionReview`):
   216→
   217→```bash
   218→uv run linkml-validate -s src/ai_gene_review/schema/gene_review.yaml -C PredictionReview genes/ECOLI/yciO/yciO-predictions-review.yaml
   219→```
   220→
   221→### Prediction sources
   222→
   223→Track predictions from: DeepECTF, PANTHER/IBA, InterPro2GO, CLEAN, GloEC, MAPred, or any
   224→other computational method that produces GO or EC predictions.
   225→
   226→### Assessment categories (from de Crécy-Lagard et al. 2025, PMID:40703034)
   227→
   228→| Code | CS | Description |
   229→|------|-----|-------------|
   230→| COR  | 2   | Correct novel prediction |
   231→| CNN  | 2   | Correct but Not Novel (already in training data) |
   232→| LSP  | 2   | Less Precise than existing annotation |
   233→| UNC  | 1   | Uncertain - cannot validate or refute |
   234→| PLI  | 0   | Paralog Incorrect - wrong paralog subfamily |
   235→| NPI  | 0   | Nonparalog Incorrect - refuted by evidence |
   236→| REP  | 0   | Repetition - frequency-biased prediction |
   237→
   238→### Error types
   239→
   240→- `PARALOG_OVERANNOTATION` - Wrong paralog subfamily (Type 6)
   241→- `PATHWAY_CONTEXT_IGNORED` - Predicted pathway absent from organism
   242→- `FREQUENCY_BIAS` - Model defaults to high-frequency training labels
   243→- `IN_VITRO_NOT_IN_VIVO` - In vitro activity doesn't match biological function
   244→- `TRAINING_DATA_CONTAMINATION` - Prediction already in training data
   245→- See schema for full list
   246→
   247→## Page rendering and deployment
   248→
   249→The site is deployed from `main` branch at root via GitHub Pages to https://ai4curation.io/ai-gene-review/.
   250→
   251→### Gene review HTML
   252→```bash
   253→just render <organism> <gene>    # render one gene
   254→just render-all                  # render all genes (~1200 files)
   255→```
   256→Output: `genes/<org>/<gene>/<gene>-ai-review.html`
   257→
   258→### Project pages
   259→Project docs live in `projects/*.md`. Render with:
   260→```bash
   261→just render-projects                          # render all
   262→ai-gene-review render-projects projects/FOO.md -o pages/projects  # render one
   263→```
   264→Output: `pages/projects/<NAME>.html`
   265→
   266→**Important:** The project index page (`pages/projects/index.html`) is **manually maintained**. When adding a new project, you must manually add a `<div class="project-card">` entry to the index HTML. The `render-projects` command does NOT update the index.
   267→
   268→### Browser app
   269→```bash
   270→just deploy-browser    # update data.js + index.html for the interactive browser
   271→```
   272→Output: `app/`
   273→
   274→### CI automation
   275→The `generate-pages` workflow runs on push to main when gene YAMLs, schema, templates, or project markdown change. It renders everything and creates a PR. Pages deploy directly from main — no gh-pages branch needed for the static content.
   276→
   277→## General guidelines
   278→
   279→* NEVER guess identifiers for terms, genes, publications. Always use the relevant tools or MCPS, or look them up in derived files.
   280→* For files `<GENE>-notes.md`, use literature deep search, and always record provenance for assertions, e.g `[PMID:12345 "<supporting text>"]`
   281→
   282→## Support code
   283→
   284→There is also support code in `src/ai_gene_review`, see the CLAUDE.md file in that directory for more details
   285→on best practices for working with the code.
   286→
   287→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it looks malicious. If it does, you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer high-level questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

content.text: Now let me examine each of the changed files to conduct a thorough review.

content.content:
1→---
     2→species: [human, mouse, DROME, worm]
     3→sidecars:
     4→  genes: MIRNA_DEGRADATION/genes.csv
     5→  fetch_queue: MIRNA_DEGRADATION/fetch_queue.csv
     6→  sources: MIRNA_DEGRADATION/sources.md
     7→---
     8→# MicroRNA Degradation / TDMD Project
     9→
    10→## Overview
    11→
    12→This is the phase-1 scaffold for reviewing dedicated gene products in metazoan microRNA degradation.
    13→The first pass is intentionally centered on target-directed microRNA degradation (TDMD), not on every
    14→process that can influence miRNA abundance.
    15→
    16→The anchor paper from Chris, Farnung et al. 2026 in Nature,
    17→["The E3 ubiquitin ligase mechanism specifying targeted microRNA degradation"](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-026-10232-0),
    18→makes the initial scope sharper: the strongest direct mechanistic evidence currently converges on the
    19→ZSWIM8-CUL3-ARIH1 axis acting on AGO-miRNA complexes bound by specialized trigger RNAs. That is the
    20→right place to start a review project without accidentally absorbing half of RNA biology.
    21→
    22→## Phase-1 Question
    23→
    24→Which dedicated proteins directly execute or specify TDMD in metazoans, and how should their GO
    25→annotations distinguish core TDMD function from generic RNA silencing, ubiquitin ligase, or RNA
    26→decay biology?
    27→
    28→## Selection Criteria
    29→
    30→- Include genes with direct genetic, biochemical, or structural evidence for participation in TDMD or immediate AGO-trigger recognition.
    31→- Prefer genes whose own protein function is meaningfully reviewable in a GO-centric gene review.
    32→- Keep AGO paralogs if there is evidence they are direct TDMD substrates or part of the immediate mechanistic target layer.
    33→- Track trigger RNAs and trigger-bearing transcripts as source/context regulators, but do not automatically turn them into phase-1 review jobs.
    34→- Defer generic RNA turnover, proteasome, or ubiquitin housekeeping factors unless a TDMD-specific role becomes part of the actual review question.
    35→
    36→## Scope Guardrails
    37→
    38→In scope for phase 1:
    39→
    40→- The ZSWIM8 ligase axis and the immediate AGO substrate layer.
    41→- Endogenous trigger RNAs and trigger-bearing transcripts as contextual regulators in project notes.
    42→
    43→Out of scope for phase 1:
    44→
    45→- General miRNA biogenesis genes such as DROSHA, DGCR8, DICER1, TARBP2, and XPO5.
    46→- Canonical effector-side repression genes such as TNRC6A, TNRC6B, TNRC6C, or CCR4-NOT components.
    47→- Generic CRL or proteasome housekeeping components such as RBX1, UBE2D2, UBE2L3, and proteasome subunits unless a TDMD-specific review need emerges.
    48→- Broad RNA decay enzymes such as DIS3L2, TUT4, TUT7, XRN factors, and Nibbler-like trimming factors. These remain phase-2 expansion candidates unless the evidence shows they are part of the core dedicated machinery for the review target in question.
    49→- Protein-coding genes whose only current relevance is a TDMD trigger site in the 3' UTR. These are biologically important but should not dominate gene-function review jobs.
    50→
    51→## Initial Core Review Queue
    52→
    53→This queue is intentionally narrow. It is the reviewable protein layer that the current evidence most
    54→strongly supports.
    55→
    56→| Gene | UniProt | Priority | Why it is in the initial queue |
    57→|------|---------|----------|--------------------------------|
    58→| ZSWIM8 | A7E2V4 | Tier 1 | Dedicated TDMD substrate receptor and central project anchor |
    59→| CUL3 | Q13618 | Tier 1 | Core cullin scaffold of the TDMD ligase |
    60→| ARIH1 | Q9Y4X5 | Tier 1 | Partner E3 required for AGO polyubiquitylation |
    61→| ELOB | Q15370 | Tier 1 | Obligatory ZSWIM8 ligase partner |
    62→| ELOC | Q15369 | Tier 1 | Obligatory ZSWIM8 ligase partner |
    63→| AGO2 | Q9UKV8 | Tier 1 | Direct mechanistic substrate in reconstitution and structural work |
    64→| AGO1 | Q9UL18 | Tier 2 | AGO family TDMD substrate layer |
    65→| AGO3 | Q9H9G7 | Tier 2 | AGO family TDMD substrate layer |
    66→| AGO4 | Q9HCK5 | Tier 2 | AGO family TDMD substrate layer |
    67→
    68→## Context Regulators Tracked But Not Queued As Initial Gene Reviews
    69→
    70→These are part of the pathway framing, but they should stay out of the phase-1 review queue unless a
    71→later subproject explicitly decides to review trigger-bearing transcripts or ncRNA regulators.
    72→
    73→- **CYRANO / OIP5-AS1**: lncRNA trigger for miR-7 and a canonical endogenous TDMD example.
    74→- **NREP**: conserved trigger-bearing transcript for miR-29b with in vivo behavioral phenotypes.
    75→- **SERPINE1**: endogenous TDMD trigger for miR-30b and miR-30c during cell-cycle re-entry.
    76→- **HSUR1**: viral prototype trigger for miR-27; important conceptually but better handled as context or a viral subproject.
    77→- **Drosophila AGO1 mRNA**: validated endogenous trigger for miR-999 and a useful comparative example.
    78→- **ATP6V1G1, LPAR4, PLAGL1, LRRC58**: mouse trigger-bearing transcripts from Lin et al. 2026; biologically important, but not dedicated TDMD machinery genes.
    79→
    80→## Deferred Expansion Modules
    81→
    82→- The TUT4/TUT7-DIS3L2 branch and other miRNA tailing or trimming factors.
    83→- Comparative species modules for fly and worm TDMD machinery once the human core queue is underway.
    84→- Viral TDMD modules as a separate subproject if those examples become important for ontology work.
    85→
    86→## Review Launch Workflow
    87→
    88→`projects/MIRNA_DEGRADATION/genes.csv` is the human-readable queue with rationale.
    89→`projects/MIRNA_DEGRADATION/fetch_queue.csv` is the headerless machine-oriented input for the CLI.
    90→
    91→```bash
    92→# Fetch the phase-1 queue
    93→uv run ai-gene-review batch-fetch projects/MIRNA_DEGRADATION/fetch_queue.csv
    94→
    95→# Launch one review
    96→just fetch-gene human ZSWIM8
    97→just deep-research human ZSWIM8 --provider perplexity
    98→just validate human ZSWIM8
    99→```
   100→
   101→## Seed Sources
   102→
   103→The source intake file lives at [projects/MIRNA_DEGRADATION/sources.md](MIRNA_DEGRADATION/sources.md).
   104→That file is the place to append new papers from Chris before the project expands the queue.
   105→
   106→## Status
   107→
   108→- [x] Project scaffold created
   109→- [x] Chris seed Nature 2026 paper integrated
   110→- [x] Initial TDMD core queue defined
   111→- [x] Batch-fetch queue prepared for review launch
   112→- [ ] Per-gene folders fetched
   113→- [ ] Per-gene reviews underway
   114→
   115→## Notes
   116→
   117→### 2026-04-04
   118→
   119→- Chris seed source integrated: Farnung et al. 2026 sharpened the decision to treat TDMD as the phase-1 scope.
   120→- Trigger RNAs and trigger-bearing transcripts are explicitly tracked, but are not automatically phase-1 review jobs because the ai-gene-review pipeline is protein and GO centric.
   121→- The first pass stays human-centered for review jobs. Comparative fly, mouse, and worm layers can be added after the core machinery reviews exist.
   122→

Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it looks malicious. If it does, you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer high-level questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1→# MicroRNA Degradation Project Sources
     2→
     3→This file tracks the seed papers that define project scope. It is intentionally smaller than a full
     4→literature review. New papers from Chris should be appended here first, and only then used to widen
     5→the review queue.
     6→
     7→## P0: Anchor Mechanism
     8→
     9→| Source | Why it matters now | Current effect on scope |
    10→|--------|--------------------|-------------------------|
    11→| [Farnung et al. 2026, "The E3 ubiquitin ligase mechanism specifying targeted microRNA degradation" (Nature)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-026-10232-0) | Direct biochemical and cryo-EM mechanism for trigger-dependent AGO recognition by the ZSWIM8-CUL3 ligase with ELOB, ELOC, and ARIH1 | Defines the phase-1 queue as a TDMD machinery project, not a generic miRNA turnover project |
    12→| [Shi et al. 2020, "The ZSWIM8 ubiquitin ligase mediates target-directed microRNA degradation" (Science)](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33184237/) | Establishes ZSWIM8 ligase requirement for TDMD and expands the biological relevance across mammals, flies, and nematodes | Justifies ZSWIM8 as the central project anchor |
    13→| [Han et al. 2020, "A ubiquitin ligase mediates target-directed microRNA decay independently of tailing and trimming" (Science)](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33184234/) | Independent TDMD mechanism paper showing that ZSWIM8-dependent decay does not require tailing and trimming to be the core explanation | Keeps TUT and trimming pathways out of phase-1 core scope |
    14→
    15→## P1: Endogenous Trigger Biology
    16→
    17→| Source | Why it matters now | Current effect on scope |
    18→|--------|--------------------|-------------------------|
    19→| [Kleaveland et al. 2018, "A Network of Noncoding Regulatory RNAs Acts in the Mammalian Brain" (Cell)](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29887379/) | Canonical endogenous CYRANO-miR-7 example linking TDMD to mammalian brain biology | Track CYRANO as context, but do not add ncRNA jobs to the phase-1 queue |
    20→| [Bitetti et al. 2018, "MicroRNA degradation by a conserved target RNA regulates animal behavior" (Nature Structural & Molecular Biology)](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29483647/) | NREP example with in vivo phenotype; useful for distinguishing validated endogenous trigger biology from speculative trigger predictions | Track NREP as context and later regulator-focused expansion |
    21→| [Ghini et al. 2018, "Endogenous transcripts control miRNA levels and activity in mammalian cells by target-directed miRNA degradation" (Nature Communications)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-018-05182-9) | SERPINE1 establishes an endogenous protein-coding trigger transcript in mammalian cells | Supports a separate "trigger-bearing transcript" context list without turning those genes into phase-1 review jobs |
    22→| [Li et al. 2023, "Screening of Drosophila microRNA-degradation sequences reveals Argonaute1 mRNA's role in regulating miR-999" (Nature Communications)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-37819-9) | Useful comparative Drosophila example and a reminder that trigger-bearing transcripts can be physiologically important | Comparative layer, not part of the initial human queue |
    23→| [Lin et al. 2026, "mRNA 3' UTRs direct microRNA degradation to participate in imprinted gene networks and regulate growth" (Genes & Development)](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41871909/) | Expands the validated mouse trigger list to ATP6V1G1, LPAR4, PLAGL1, and LRRC58 | Track these genes as regulators/context only unless a later subproject is explicitly about trigger-bearing transcripts |
    24→
    25→## P2: Deferred Expansion
    26→
    27→| Source | Why it matters now | Current effect on scope |
    28→|--------|--------------------|-------------------------|
    29→| [Yang et al. 2020, "AGO-bound mature miRNAs are oligouridylated by TUTs and subsequently degraded by DIS3L2" (Nature Communications)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-020-16533-w) | Strong evidence for a miRNA turnover branch involving tailing and DIS3L2 | Keep this branch as phase-2 expansion rather than mixing it into the phase-1 TDMD machinery queue |
    30→
    31→## Intake Rule
    32→
    33→- If a new paper directly changes the identity of the dedicated TDMD machinery, update `genes.csv` and `fetch_queue.csv`.
    34→- If a new paper adds trigger RNAs or trigger-bearing transcripts, update this file and the context-regulator section of `MIRNA_DEGRADATION.md` first.
    35→- Do not widen the review queue just because a gene's transcript contains a TDMD trigger site.
    36→
